elements.sent-2411-001: We introduce \ourmod, a neural network predictor that combines Convolutional and Transformer architectures with Positional encodings, for neutral atomic hydrogen (\hi) spectral analysis.
elements.sent-2411-002: Trained on synthetic datasets, our models predict cold neutral gas fraction (\FCNM) and \hi\ opacity correction factor (\RHI) from emission spectra based on the learned relationships between the desired output parameters and observables (optically-thin column density and peak brightness).
elements.sent-2411-003: As a follow-up to Murray et al. (2020)’s shallow Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), we construct deep CNN models and compare them to \ourmod\ models.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our findings highlight the robustness of the proposed model with sinusoidal positional encoding applied directly to the spectral input, addressing perturbations in training dataset shuffling and convolutional network weight initializations.
elements.sent-2411-005: Higher spectral resolutions with increased spectral channels offer advantages, albeit with increased training time.
elements.sent-2411-006: Diverse synthetic datasets enhance model performance and generalization, as demonstrated by producing \FCNM\ and \RHI\ values consistent with evaluation ground truths.
elements.sent-2411-007: Applications of \ourmod\ to observed emission data reveal strong agreement between the predictions and Gaussian decomposition-based estimates (from emission and absorption surveys), emphasizing its potential in \hi\ spectral analysis.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our training spectral datasets are generated based on Hydrodynamic and Magnetohydrodynamic simulations from \cite{Saury2014 (resolution $\sim$ 0.04 pc) and \cite{Seta2022 (resolution $\sim$ 0.4 pc), respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: We generate synthetic \hi\ observations considering the full radiative transfer of the 21-cm line (described in \citealt{Marchal2019 ) by using density, temperature, and velocity fields produced by simulations.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Here, we account for thermal and turbulent motions to accurately simulate emission line broadenings.
elements.sent-2411-011: The spectral line widths and shapes arise directly from the intrinsic physical properties of the simulated medium.
elements.sent-2411-012: We do not decompose the synthetic spectra into Gaussian components; instead, we estimate their full widths at half maximum (FWHM) using second-moment analysis.
elements.sent-2411-013: The resulting line widths in our training dataset span from approximately 0.5 \kms\ to 44 \kms.
elements.sent-2411-014: Additionally, in order to replicate real observations, we introduce random noise at levels of 0.5 -- 0.8 K to synthetic spectra and smooth the synthetic data cubes with a Gaussian beam size of 45 arcsec.
elements.sent-2411-015: We then compute the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) based on the typical peak intensities of the spectra, which generally fall between 3 K and 100 K.
elements.sent-2411-016: This results in an estimated S/N ratio ranging from $\sim$5:1 to 150:1, depending on both the specific line strength and the level of added noise.
elements.sent-2411-017: Our final training database comprises five position-position-velocity (PPV) cubes, each with dimensions of (512 $\times$ 512 $\times$ N), where N indicates the number of velocity channels.
elements.sent-2411-018: Using two velocity resolutions: 0.3125 and 0.8 \kms, corresponding to 256 and 101 velocity channels, respectively, we can evaluate the impact of spectral resolutions on our predictive outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-019: Every position pixel in these cubes is associated with an emission spectrum (brightness temperature $T_\mathrm{b (v)$ in Kelvin) and an absorption spectrum (optical depth $\tau(v)$), both as a function of Doppler velocity $v$ in km\ s$^{-1}$.}

elements.sent-2411-091: Our supervised machine-learning task aims to quantify optically-thick cold \hi\ gas (\FCNM\ and \RHI) from emission spectra using deep learning models.
elements.sent-2411-092: Models are trained on synthetic spectral cubes with known ground truth labels.
elements.sent-2411-093: During training, models optimize their parameters -- weights and biases -- by minimizing prediction errors, with the Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) chosen as the evaluation metric.
elements.sent-2411-094: We tune hyperparameters and configurations for effective generalization.
elements.sent-2411-095: Once trained and validated, models make predictions on unseen data.
elements.sent-2411-096: We have designed deep CNNs and Transformer-based \ourmods\ for this regression task.
elements.sent-2411-097: CNNs use convolutional layers to learn patterns within the spectral input, while \ourmods\ combine CNN and Transformer, leveraging multi-head attention for long-range interactions between different parts of emission spectra.
elements.sent-2411-098: Refer to Appendix \ref{sec:models for details of the two deep learning architectures.}
elements.sent-2411-099: Moreover, full descriptions of the experimental setup, training procedures, and optimized hyperparameters (e.g., number of layers, learning rates) for both models are available in Appendix \ref{sec:hyperparams .}

elements.sent-2411-215: The Fourier transform operation converts an \hi\ spectrum (brightness temperature as a function of velocity) into a power spectrum in the wave-number domain ($k_v$).
elements.sent-2411-216: In the context of analyzing \hi\ spectra, where the line broadening of the \hi\ features are linked to thermal and non-thermal processes, we can simplify the interpretation of $k_v$ as representing the ``scale'' of patterns/structures within the spectral data.
elements.sent-2411-217: A higher $k_v$ value aligns with higher spectral frequencies, indicating finer colder details (CNM), whereas a lower $k_v$ value corresponds to lower spectral frequencies, reflecting larger-scale warmer structures (WNM).
elements.sent-2411-218: This interpretation allows to define a threshold wave number $k_v$ to distinguish between CNM and WNM components.
elements.sent-2411-219: Since $k_v$ reflects the scales in spectral widths, a proxy for \hi\ temperatures, it is often chosen finely enough to confidently represent CNM structures.
elements.sent-2411-220: In high density regions of CNM (n $\gtrsim$ 100 cm$^{-3}$, e.g., \citealt{Shaw2017 ), collisions (among \hi\ atoms themselves and between \hi\ atoms with electrons, ions) dominate the excitation of \hi\ atoms, spin temperature \Ts\ is thus close to the kinetic temperature \Tk\ \citep[e.g.,][]{Kulkarni1988,Liszt2001 .}}
elements.sent-2411-221: Observationally, observers generally assume \Ts\ $\approx$ \Tk\ for temperatures up to 1000 K \citep{McClure-Griffiths2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-222: In order to detect CNM components characterized by a Gaussian with a line-width $\Delta V_\text{FWHM}$ $\sim$ 7 \kms (standard deviation width $\sigma$ = 3 \kms), \citealt{Marchal2024 selected a wave number threshold at $k_{v,\text{thresh }$ = 0.12 (km s$^{-1}$)$^{-1}$ for their Fourier-transformed method.}}
elements.sent-2411-223: This threshold corresponds to gas with a maximum kinetic temperature of \Tkmax\ $\sim$ 1000 K, and only rescues a portion $t \sim$ 0.05 of its true total mass.
elements.sent-2411-224: As a result, the CNM fraction estimated from the Fourier transform provides a lower limit while still capturing the CNM spatial structure of large-scale \hi\ emission.

elements.sent-2411-301: In continuation of \cite{Murray2020 work of CNN models, we introduced a novel deep-learning approach, \ourmod\ models, for \hi\ spectral analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-302: These models combine CNN and Transformer architectures and have been trained on synthetic datasets generated from simulations to predict cold \hi\ gas fraction (\FCNM) and \hi\ opacity correction factor (\RHI) from emission spectra.
elements.sent-2411-303: Recognizing the importance of spectral channel order in training the deep-learning models, we experiment with different positional encoding methods.
elements.sent-2411-304: To evaluate the effectiveness of our models, we conduct a comparative analysis between CNN and \ourmod\ architectures (with positional encodings), based on testing RMSE values.
elements.sent-2411-305: Subsequently, we apply these trained models to the evaluation and observed spectral data.
elements.sent-2411-306: In summary, our key findings are as follows: -- \ourmod\ outperforms CNNs on the synthetic evaluation set, with an average improvement of 10\% in testing accuracy, convergence speed, and model training stability.
elements.sent-2411-307: While the predicted values follow well the relationships (e.g., \FCNM, \RHI\ vs \NHIthin, \TBpeak and equivalent width) learned from the synthetic training database across the entire ranges, absorption-based estimates agree with the trends only at low \NHIthin\ (and low $\tau_\text{HI}$).
elements.sent-2411-308: In general, \ourmod\ appears to deliver better performance on observed data sets compared to the CNN architectures.
elements.sent-2411-309: We propose here the \ourmod\ (a hybrid convolutional-Transformer model) and ``add sinusoidal'' positional encoding (adding sinusoidal function to the original spectra) for predicting the CNM gas fraction and \hi\ opacity effect as it is the most robust against various perturbations we expect to see in real data (shuffling schemes, convolutional neural network initialization, spectral input length).
elements.sent-2411-310: Application of the trained models to a synthetic evaluation spectral cube demonstrates \ourmod\ capability to consistently reproduce \FCNM\ and \RHI\ compared to ground truths.
elements.sent-2411-311: This consistency extends to correlations between predicted quantities and \NHI, \TBpeak, equivalent width, and \taupeak.

elements.sent-2411-333: Convolutional Neural Networks \citep[CNNs,][]{Lecun1998,fukushima1980 are a type of neural network models widely employed in deep learning, specifically tailored to process grid-like data such as images, audio spectrograms, and time series data.}
elements.sent-2411-334: These networks operate in a feed-forward manner, in which the output from one layer serves as the input to the next layer.
elements.sent-2411-335: The key idea behind CNNs is to learn hierarchical representations of features directly from the input data.
elements.sent-2411-336: A typical CNN architecture consists of multiple layers starting with an input layer, followed by hidden layers, fully connected layers, and an output layer.
elements.sent-2411-337: The input layer receives the raw data, such as images or sequences, and passes it to the subsequent layers.
elements.sent-2411-338: In image processing, the pixel values from a small rectangular region (or patch) within an image are referred to as the receptive field.
elements.sent-2411-339: Similarly, in the context of a 1D CNN applied to spectral data, the receptive field represents a segment of input values that affect the output of a specific neuron in the network.
elements.sent-2411-340: Hidden layers comprise convolutional, activation, and (optional) pooling layers.
elements.sent-2411-341: Convolutional layers apply convolution operations to the input data, where small filter(s) (a set of kernels with the same size) slide over the input grid and compute dot products to extract hierarchical features.
elements.sent-2411-342: These layers are followed by non-linear activation functions (e.g., tanh, sigmoid, softmax, or rectified linear unit) to introduce non-linearity into the network and facilitate the learning of complex relationships in the data.
elements.sent-2411-343: Optionally, pooling layers may follow to perform vector-to-scalar transformation on local regions of the input, aiding in feature extraction, reducing computational cost, and preventing overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-344: Convolutional layers are essentially made up of neurons.
elements.sent-2411-345: In each convolutional layer, neurons receive inputs from neurons in the previous layer.
elements.sent-2411-346: Each input ($x$) is assigned a random weight ($w$), and a dot product operation is performed between these weights and the corresponding input values, followed by an addition of a bias term ($b$).
elements.sent-2411-347: The resulting scalar output is passed through a non-linear activation function ($f$).
elements.sent-2411-348: Thus, the output of a single neuron ($z$) is a result of a non-linear transformation and can be mathematically expressed as: \begin{equation}z = f(w \ast x) = f(\mathbf{w^\mathrm{T}} \mathbf{x} + b) = f \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot x_i + b \right) \label{eq:activ}\end{equation} where ``$\ast$'' represents the convolution operator, $\mathbf{w}$ is the weight vector associated with the input vector $\mathbf{x}$ for a receptive field, and $n$ represents kernel size.
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}z = f(w \ast x) = f(\mathbf{w^T} x + b) = f \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot x_i + b \right) \label{eq:activ}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-349: Similar to the convolutional layers, fully connected layers (also known as dense layers) consist of neurons.
elements.sent-2411-350: These layers connect every neuron in one layer to every neuron in the next layer.
elements.sent-2411-351: One or more fully connected layers may be used, towards the end of the network, to perform high-level reasoning and classification based on the learned features.
elements.sent-2411-352: Finally, the output layer produces the final predictions or classifications by using an appropriate activation function (e.g., softmax for classification or linear for regression).
elements.sent-2411-353: In a supervised machine learning task, the network must be trained using a labeled training database, where the ground-truth outputs corresponding to the inputs are known, to determine the optimal model parameters (i.e., weights and biases) at each neuron's input across layers.
elements.sent-2411-354: The training process of a CNN model typically involves several key steps.
elements.sent-2411-355: First, the network is initialized with random values for the weights and biases.
elements.sent-2411-356: Then, input data is fed through the network, resulting in a predicted output.
elements.sent-2411-357: This predicted output is compared to the ground truth using a predefined loss function to compute the loss.
elements.sent-2411-358: Through back-propagation, the gradients of the loss function with respect to the model parameters are computed.
elements.sent-2411-359: These gradients are then used in gradient descent to adjust the weights and biases, aiming to minimize the loss and improve model performance.
elements.sent-2411-360: This iterative process continues with different batches (small subsets) of training data until convergence or a predefined stopping criterion is met.
elements.figure-2411-018: \includegraphics[width=.475\textwidth]{fig/1dcnn.png} \caption{Our CNN architecture with 8 convolutional layers using Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) activation function: $f(x) = \text{max}(0,x)$. The raw spectrum input is position encoded before being passed to the CNN. Two kernel sizes, (1$\times$7) and (1$\times$33), are iteratively employed, with the number of filters reduced by 8 units after each convolutional layer. The model outputs two desired parameters: cold \hi\ gas fraction \FCNM\ and opacity correction factor \RHI.} \label{fig:1dcnn}
elements.sent-2411-361: Following \cite{Murray2020 's CNN for \hi\ emission analysis, we adopt a typical CNN design with input, convolutional, activation, fully-connected, and output layers.}
elements.sent-2411-362: Our CNN architecture is illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:1dcnn .}
elements.sent-2411-363: The input layer accepts either the raw synthetic brightness spectrum or the position-encoded spectrum (see Appendix \ref{sec:data_repr for details on positional encoding techniques).}
elements.sent-2411-364: The width (number of neurons) dynamically adjusts for each convolutional layer based on the total number of layers in the model, with each subsequent layer having fewer neurons than the preceding one.
elements.sent-2411-365: In our approach, we iteratively employ two kernel sizes, 7 and 33, for the convolutional layers to reduce input dimensionality.
elements.sent-2411-366: This architectural choice, combined with varying kernel sizes, is expected to enable the network to learn features across various spectral scales, such as those of CNM (typical width FWHM $\lesssim$ 3 \kms) and WNM (FWHM $\sim$ 10 \kms).

elements.sent-2411-368: Batch normalization normalizes the inputs of a layer by subtracting the batch mean and dividing by the batch standard deviation.

elements.sent-2411-372: The Transformer is a deep learning architecture introduced by \cite{Vaswani2017 used for sequence modeling tasks, such as natural language processing (NLP) and time series analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-373: Unlike traditional recurrent neural networks (RNNs) and convolutional neural networks (CNNs), Transformers rely on self-attention mechanisms to capture long-range dependencies within input sequences efficiently \citep[e.g.,][]{Zhang2020,Gulati2020,Pan2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-374: A typical Transformer architecture is illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:trans_encoder_decoder and will be briefly discussed below, though refer to the original paper by \cite{Vaswani2017 for more details.}}
elements.figure-2411-019: \includegraphics[width=.5\textwidth]{fig/transformer.png} \caption{A typical architecture of the Transformer deep learning model designed for natural language processing \citep[taken from][]{Vaswani2017}. The architecture comprises two main components: encoder stack (Nx identical encoder layers), and decoder stack (Nx identical decoder layers), incorporating embedding, multi-head self-attention, positional encoding, feedforward, and ``add \& norm'' (residual connections and layer normalization) layers.} \label{fig:trans_encoder_decoder}
elements.sent-2411-375: In the context of natural language processing, sequence data is composed of tokens, which are its fundamental units of input.
elements.sent-2411-376: Tokens often represent words or subwords that form a sentence or a piece of text.
elements.sent-2411-377: However, tokens can also denote other types of entities or elements in different types of sequences, such as time series data or spectral data.
elements.sent-2411-378: At the core of the Transformer architecture is the self-attention mechanism, which allows the model to weigh the importance of different tokens within a sequence when making predictions.
elements.sent-2411-379: The input sequence is first embedded into a continuous vector space using learned embedding matrices, which convert each token into a high-dimensional representation vector (with a length of D features).
elements.sent-2411-380: To account for the sequential order of tokens, positional encoding is added to the input embeddings (data matrix $\mathbf{X \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times D}$ of N tokens and D features) before they are fed into the self-attention mechanism.}}
elements.sent-2411-381: This positional encoding typically involves adding directly sinusoidal functions of different frequencies to the embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-382: The combination of token embedding and positional encoding provides the Transformer model with a comprehensive understanding of both the content and the sequential order of the input sequence.
elements.sent-2411-383: The self-attention mechanism in the Transformer operates on three key matrices: the query matrix Q $\in \mathbb{R ^{N \times D}$, the key matrix K $\in \mathbb{R ^{N \times D}$, and the value matrix V $\in \mathbb{R ^{N \times D}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-384: These matrices are derived from the input embeddings ($\mathbf{X}$) via the weight matrices: \begin{equation}\begin{split} \mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}^{(q)} \mathbf{K} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}^{(k)} \mathbf{V} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{W}^{(v)} \label{} \end{split}\end{equation} where the matrices $\mathbf{W ^{(q)}$, $\mathbf{W ^{(q)}$ and $\mathbf{W ^{(q)}$ represent the parameters that are learned during the training.}}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation} Q = XW^{(q)} K = XW^{(k)} V = XW^{(v)} \label{} \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-385: The dimensionalities of the matrices $\mathbf{W ^{(q)}$, $\mathbf{W ^{(k)}$, and $\mathbf{W ^{(v)}$ are ($D \times D_q$), ($D \times D_k$), and ($D \times D_v$), respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-386: The two matrices $\mathbf{W ^{(q)}$ and $\mathbf{W ^{(k)}$ must have the same dimensionalities to form dot products between query and key vectors, hence $D_q = D_k$.}}
elements.sent-2411-387: The Q, K, and V matrices are used to calculate attention scores between each pair of tokens in the sequence.
elements.sent-2411-388: The attention scores determine how much each token attends to other tokens in the sequence.
elements.sent-2411-389: The attention scores are then normalized using the softmax function to obtain attention weights, which are applied to the value vectors to compute the output Y of the self-attention mechanism: \begin{equation}\mathbf{Y} = \text{attention}(\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}) = \text{softmax}\left[ \frac{\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^{\text{T}}}{\sqrt{D_\mathrm{k}}}\right] \mathbf{V} \label{}\end{equation} The Transformer also employs multi-head self-attention (MHSA), where the self-attention mechanism is applied multiple times in parallel, each with its own set of learned parameters.
elements.sent-2411-390: This allows the model to attend to different aspects of the input sequence simultaneously, capturing both local and global dependencies more effectively.
elements.sent-2411-391: In addition to self-attention layers, the Transformer architecture includes feedforward neural networks and ``add \& norm'' layers.
elements.sent-2411-392: The feedforward layers apply linear transformations followed by non-linear activation functions to the output of the self-attention layers, enabling the model to capture complex patterns and relationships within the sequence.
elements.sent-2411-393: The ``add \& norm'' layers then perform residual connections and layer normalization, stabilizing the training process and improving the flow of information through the network.
elements.sent-2411-394: In a typical Transformer architecture, the input sequence is processed by an encoder-decoder structure, with each component containing multiple stacks of MHSA, feedforward, and ``add \& norm'' layers.
elements.sent-2411-395: The encoder encodes the input sequence into a set of context-rich representations.
elements.sent-2411-396: The decoder then uses these representations, along with additional self-attention layers and cross-attention layers, to generate the output sequence token by token.
elements.sent-2411-397: Representation learning in machine learning, also referred to as feature learning, involves algorithms extracting meaningful patterns from raw data to generate representations (or features) that are easier to process.
elements.sent-2411-398: Representation learning serves as a powerful tool for gaining deeper insights into the inherent patterns, trends, and structures within the data \citep[e.g.,][]{Bengio2012,Goodfellow2016,Caron2020,Uelwer2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-399: In this study, we introduce, \ourmod, a representation learning neural network, tailored for processing \hi\ emission analysis.
elements.sent-2411-400: This model draws inspiration from both convolution and Transformer architectures.
elements.sent-2411-401: Models that use either convolution or Transformer self-attention individually have their own limitations.
elements.sent-2411-402: While convolutional neural networks excel in capturing short-range features within the input data via local receptive fields \citep{Sainath2013,AbdelHamid2014,Li2019CNN,Kriman2019,Han2020 , they need many more layers or parameters to extract global information.}
elements.sent-2411-403: The Transformer self-attention, on the other hand, specializes in revealing the intricate long-range interactions; yet, they may struggle with extracting fine-grained local feature patterns \citep[e.g.,][]{Zhang2020,Gulati2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-404: Recent research has demonstrated that combining convolution and self-attention enhances performance over utilizing them independently.
elements.sent-2411-405: With this combined method, the model can learn both position-wise local characteristics and global interactions \citep{Bello2019,Lu2019Trans,Yang2019CNNTRANS,Yu2018CNNTrans,Wu2020Trans,Pan2022,Rozanski2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-406: Since both global and local interactions within frequency/velocity spectral channels are crucial for model generalization, given their relation to the kinematical scales of \hi\ phases, we design \ourmod\ via a combination of CNN (representation learning block) and Transformer self-attention mechanism (prediction block).
elements.sent-2411-407: CNNs use convolutional layers to extract compact features within the emission spectra.
elements.sent-2411-408: These features are then transformed into an embedding matrix input before being passed to the multi-head attention of a Transformer.
elements.sent-2411-409: The intuition is that the deep CNN learns patterns from \hi\ spectra, and the Transformer's attention mechanism enables long-range interactions between different parts of the spectra to predict the cold gas fraction \FCNM\ and opacity correction \RHI.
elements.sent-2411-410: Our investigation also encompasses an evaluation of the influence of positional encodings on the models' performance.
elements.figure-2411-020: \includegraphics[width=.5\textwidth]{fig/ctrans.png} \caption{A basic architecture of our \ourmod\ model: CNN on the left branch and a stack of Nx identical Transformer decoder layers on the right. The feature vector with length $n$ from the CNN output is converted into a $m \times k$ embedding matrix (containing m tokens, each of length k, hence $n = m \times k$).} \label{fig:cnn_transformer}
elements.sent-2411-411: Figure \ref{fig:cnn_transformer illustrates the basic design of our \ourmod.}
elements.sent-2411-412: The initial input to the CNN is either the original spectrum or a position-encoded spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-413: The last fully-connected layer of the CNN outputs a feature vector with a length $n$, which is then transformed into a matrix with dimensions of $m \times k$ to mimic an embedding matrix (with $m$ tokens, each with a length of $k$, so $n = m \times k$).
elements.sent-2411-414: We experiment with different shapes for the embedding matrix and yield comparable RMSEs with a range of $m$ and $k$ values.
elements.sent-2411-415: This is likely because the embedding matrix, regardless of its size, contains the same information as the 1D feature vector.
elements.sent-2411-416: We then opt for an ``arbitrary'' length of $k = 9$ for our Transformer's embedding input.
elements.sent-2411-417: Next, each token (or row) in the embedding matrix is position-encoded using a series of sine and cosine functions with gradually increasing wavelength before being given to the Transformer decoder (see \cite{Vaswani2017 for the choice of embedding sinusoidal positional encoding).}
elements.sent-2411-418: Note that the embedding positional encoding is applied to the embedding matrix input of the Transformer.
elements.sent-2411-419: This positional embedding is different from the initial positional encodings applied to the original spectra.
elements.sent-2411-420: Finally, the Transformer decoder will predict the two \hi\ quantities of interest: \FCNM\ and \RHI.
elements.sent-2411-421: In the context of our supervised regression task, where we simultaneously predict two quantities: cold gas fraction and opacity correction factor, we opt for the Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) as the model evaluation metric throughout this work.
elements.sent-2411-422: RMSE quantifies the difference between ground truths (\FCNM\ and \RHI) and their corresponding predictions ($\hat{f _{\text{CNM }$ and $\hat{\mathcal{R _{\text{HI }$) values, providing an error measurement in the same units as the target variables.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-423: The overall RMSE for the two quantities is calculated as: \begin{small \begin{equation}\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2K} \left(\sum_{i=1}^{K} (f_{\text{CNM},i} - \hat{f}_{\text{CNM},i})^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{K} (\mathcal{R}_{\text{HI},i} - \hat{\mathcal{R}}_{\text{HI},i})^2 \right)} \label{eq:rmse}\end{equation} \end{small where $K$ is the sample size, and the subscripts i index individual data points.}}
elements.sent-2411-424: We use four spectral cubes in the training database for training and reserve the fifth for the final evaluation phase.
elements.sent-2411-425: To facilitate our assessment, we split the training set of 1,048,576 spectra into three subsets: 60\% for training (629,146 spectra), 20\% for validation (209,715), and 20\% for testing (209,715).
elements.sent-2411-426: For the training split above, we experiment with ten random initial weights for each neural network architecture, and train for 60 epochs.
elements.sent-2411-427: Our model design is such that it operates on a spectrum-by-spectrum basis.
elements.sent-2411-428: The initial step is to rearrange the PPV spectral cubes into an array of individual spectra.
elements.sent-2411-429: Positional encodings are then applied to the original spectra.
elements.sent-2411-430: Subsequently, these position-encoded spectra arrays, along with their associated \FCNM\ and \RHI, proceed to a data-loading procedure before being fed into the models as batches of samples (i.e., subsets of the training set).
elements.sent-2411-431: During the training phase, we use a batch size of 256 samples, RMSE as the loss function to minimize the difference between the predictions and ground truths, and Adam optimizer to adjust model parameters (weights and biases).
elements.sent-2411-432: Additionally, we employ the early stopping technique to continuously assess the models' performance on a validation dataset throughout the training process, aiming to avert overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-433: Whenever the performance on the validation dataset stops improving or starts to degrade, early stopping interrupts training to prevent the model from learning the noise in the training data, which may lead to overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-434: The weights of the model achieving the best performance on the validation sets are then saved for the testing phase.
elements.figure-2411-021: \includegraphics[width=0.475\textwidth]{fig/cnn_nlayers.png} \caption{Swarm-box plots for training (top), validation (middle), and testing (bottom) RMSE values from ten-trial training sessions of CNN models with different numbers of convolutional layers (using the original spectral data, i.e. without positional encoding). Median training/validation RMSE values are computed over 60 epochs for each trial, while testing RMSE values are obtained individually from each trial. The y-axis scales in all panels are identical for better direct comparisons.} \label{fig:cnn_nlayers}
elements.sent-2411-435: In the first experiment, we seek the optimal parameters of CNNs for further processing.
elements.sent-2411-436: Figure \ref{fig:cnn_nlayers presents swarm-box plots of the testing and validation RMSE obtained from ten random initial weights for the CNN models with varying numbers of layers.}
elements.sent-2411-437: A clear understanding emerges regarding the observed trends in the relationship between the number of convolutional layers and model performance.
elements.sent-2411-438: Although the RMSE values consistently decrease in the training phase with the addition of more convolutional layers, both testing and validation RMSEs show no substantial improvement beyond eight layers.
elements.sent-2411-439: This pattern strongly indicates overfitting in complex CNN architectures, especially with a higher number of convolutional layers ($N > 10$ layers) and underfitting with shallower CNN architectures ($N < 6$ layers).
elements.sent-2411-440: Moreover, the validation/testing RMSE values in cases of overfitting and underfitting show considerable scatter.
elements.sent-2411-441: Once the number of layers surpasses eight, adding more convolutional layers does not significantly yield any further enhancements in model performance.
elements.sent-2411-442: In addition, the swarm plots indicate a significant increase in RMSE standard deviations across different initial conditions after ten convolutional layers.
elements.sent-2411-443: It is worth noting that increasing the number of convolutional layers in a CNN greatly extends training time.
elements.sent-2411-444: A 14-layer CNN, for example, requires approximately three times the training time of a 10-layer CNN, and about six times the training time of an 8-layer CNN.
elements.sent-2411-445: Considering a balance between model complexity, training efficiency, and accuracy, we will utilize an 8-layer CNN for processing our spectral data.
elements.sent-2411-446: We then focus on the learning rate -- a hyperparameter that determines how much a neural network weight is adjusted using backpropagation with respect to the amount of RMSE between the predicted values and labels.
elements.sent-2411-447: After experimenting with multiple learning rate values on training sets with 256 (101) velocity channels, we determine the optimal learning rates to be $5 \times 10^{-3}$ ($5 \times 10^{-2}$) for deep CNNs and $5 \times 10^{-3}$ ($9 \times 10^{-3}$) for \ourmods.
elements.sent-2411-448: We will consistently use these learning rates and an 8-layer CNN architecture throughout all subsequent experiments.
elements.figure-2411-022: \fontsize{10}{9}\selectfont \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.25} \begin{tabular}{l| l| l} Abbreviation Representation Input shape [0.5ex] 1. original Original spectrum ($1 \times N$) 2. concat\_index Append index array alongside original spectrum ($2 \times N$) 3. add\_index Add index array to original spectrum ($1 \times N$) 4. concat\_sinusoidal Append index sinusoidal function alongside original spectrum ($2 \times N$) 5. add\_sinusoidal Add index sinusoidal function to original spectrum ($1 \times N$) 6. concat\_polynomial Append index 2$^\mathrm{nd}$ polynomial function alongside original spectrum ($2 \times N$) 7. learnable$^{*}$ Add learnable positional encoding to original spectrum ($1 \times N$) [1ex] \end{tabular} \caption{Positional encodings for 1D spectral data of $N$ channels. ~~~~\\\\ {$^{(*)}$} Learnable positional encoding is only used in \ourmods.} \label{table:pe}
elements.sent-2411-449: In the analysis of sequence-like data, such as spectral data, the order of spectral channels is indeed crucial.
elements.sent-2411-450: Each position in the spectrum corresponds to a signal intensity value at a specific frequency (i.e., wavelength, or velocity channel), and the arrangement of these positions conveys meaningful information about the underlying processes.
elements.sent-2411-451: Providing information about the order of elements in a sequence using positional encodings empowers the model to capture dependencies and patterns associated with the order of the data, resulting in enhanced performance.
elements.sent-2411-452: Positional encodings have proven effective in sequence-to-sequence models and natural language processing, particularly in contexts where the order of words has a great impact on semantics \cite[][]{Vaswani2017,Gehring2017,Devlin2018,Radford2019,Raffel2019, Lan2019, Yang2019,Liu2019,Ke2020,Dufter2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-453: In this work, we use and evaluate different positional encoding variants.
elements.sent-2411-454: For a given original input vector x (e.g., a spectrum), we will construct its corresponding positional encoding vector r.
elements.sent-2411-455: Then, we will combine the vector r with the input vector x element-wise.
elements.sent-2411-456: We can either add (``add'') or concatenate (``concat'') the positional encoding vector to the original input vector.
elements.sent-2411-457: However, it is important to note that concatenating will increase the dimensionality of the input space, potentially leading to a significant increase in computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-458: This work employs a total of seven positional encoding techniques: ``original spectrum'', ``concat index'', ``add index'', ``concat sinusoidal'', ``add sinusoidal'', ``concat second--order polynomial'' and ``(add) learnable''.
elements.sent-2411-459: These techniques are all drawn from absolute positional encodings which provide information about the absolute position or order of elements in a sequence.
elements.sent-2411-460: Table \ref{table:pe provides an overview of the designed positional encodings, and each technique is further elaborated below.}
elements.sent-2411-461: This data representation uses the original spectrum (i.e. without positional encoding), which represents a 1D spectrum characterized by the brightness temperature $T_\mathrm{b}$ (measured in Kelvin) as a function of velocity $v$ (in $km~s^{-1}$).
elements.sent-2411-462: In this context, the model input has a size of ($1 \times N$), comprising one row and $N$ columns, where $N$ is the number of spectral channels (either 256 or 101 in this work).
elements.sent-2411-463: Serving as the foundational representation, the ``original spectrum'' requires no data preprocessing.
elements.sent-2411-464: The model input is given by: \begin{equation}\mathbf{x_\mathrm{original}} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N) \in \mathbb{R}^N \label{pe:original}\end{equation} where $x_{i}$ corresponds to the brightness temperature in the $i^{th}$ velocity channel.
elements.subsection-2411-014: Concatenating index PE to original spectrum
elements.sent-2411-465: In this positional encoding technique, a positional encoding vector (PEV) is generated to match the size of the original vector ($1 \times N$).
elements.sent-2411-466: Each element in the positional encoding vector is calculated as the integer index number divided by $N$, resulting in values within the range of 0 to 1. \begin{equation}\mathbf{x_\mathrm{index, c}} = (c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_N) = (\frac{0}{N}, \frac{1}{N}, \ldots, \frac{N}{N}) \in \mathbb{R}^N \label{pe:indexc}\end{equation} This approach introduces a fixed absolute positional encoding, where the values of the PEV directly represent the position of each element in the original spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-467: The absolute positional encoding provides the model with explicit information about the absolute order of elements in the sequence.
elements.sent-2411-468: After generating the positional encoding vector, it is concatenated with the original vector along the velocity axis.
elements.sent-2411-469: The resulting matrix has a size of ($2 \times N$), where the first row represents the original spectrum $\mathbf{x_\mathrm{original = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N)$, and the second row contains the PEV values $\mathbf{x_\mathrm{index,c = (c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_N)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-470: The model input is given by the following matrix: \begin{equation}\mathbf{X}_\mathrm{index,c} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{x_\mathrm{original}} \mathbf{x_\mathrm{index,c}} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_{1} x_{2} \ldots x_{N} 0/N 1/N \ldots N/N \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times N}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}X_index,c =  \mathbf{x_original} \mathbf{x_index,c}  =  x_{1} x_{2} \ldots x_{N} 0/N 1/N \ldots N/N  \in R^{2 \times N}\end{equation}
elements.subsection-2411-015: Adding index PE to original spectrum
elements.sent-2411-471: Following the same approach as the ``concatenating index PE'' technique, a positional encoding vector is also created with the same size as the original vector ($1 \times N$).
elements.sent-2411-472: Subsequently, the positional encoding vector is directly element--wise added to the original spectrum $\mathbf{x_\mathrm{original = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N)$, resulting in a new vector with the same size as the original spectrum ($1 \times N$).}}
elements.sent-2411-473: This method enhances the original spectrum with positional information for improved model understanding.
elements.sent-2411-474: The final model input is represented by the following vector: \begin{equation}\mathbf{x_\mathrm{index,a}} = (x_1 + \frac{0}{N}, x_2 + \frac{1}{N}, \ldots, x_N + \frac{N}{N}) \in \mathbb{R}^N\end{equation}.
elements.subsection-2411-016: Concatenating sinusoidal PE to original spectrum
elements.sent-2411-475: Presenting the ``concatenating sinusoidal PE to original spectrum'' technique, inspired by Transformer positional encodings \citep{Vaswani2017,Dehghani2018,Li2019Trans , which utilize sinusoidal and cosine functions for generating the positional encoding matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-476: In the context of spectral data, unlike the Transformer's input, which consists of multiple vectors representing words, our input format involves a single spectral vector.
elements.sent-2411-477: To adapt, we simplify the positional encoding formula expression: \begin{equation}\mathbf{x_\mathrm{sin, c}} = sin(0, 1, \ldots, N) \in \mathbb{R}^N \label{pe:indexc}\end{equation} After being generated, the positional encoding vector $\mathbf{x_\mathrm{sin, c }$ is concatenated with the original vector along the velocity axis.}
elements.sent-2411-478: The resulting matrix has a size of ($2 \times N$), with the first row representing the original spectrum $\mathbf{x_\mathrm{original = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N)$, and the second row containing the positional encoding vector values $\mathbf{x_\mathrm{sin, c = sin(0, 1, \ldots, N)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-479: The final model input is thus represented by the following matrix: \begin{equation}\mathbf{X}_\mathrm{sin,c} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{x_\mathrm{original}} \mathbf{x_\mathrm{sin,c}} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_{1} x_{2} \ldots x_{N} sin(0) sin(1) \ldots sin(N) \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times N}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation}X_sin,c =  \mathbf{x_original} \mathbf{x_sin,c}  =  x_{1} x_{2} \ldots x_{N} sin(0) sin(1) \ldots sin(N)  \in R^{2 \times N}\end{equation}
elements.subsection-2411-017: Adding sinusoidal PE to original spectrum
elements.sent-2411-480: Introducing the ``adding sinusoidal PE to original spectrum'' technique, which adopts the same approach as the ``concatenating sinusoidal PE'' technique for generating the positional encoding vector.
elements.sent-2411-481: However, in this PE technique, we then perform element-wise addition of the positional encoding vector to the original spectrum $\mathbf{x_\mathrm{original = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-482: This results in a new vector of the same size as the original spectrum ($1 \times N$).
elements.sent-2411-483: The ultimate input to be fed into the model is represented by the following vector: \begin{equation}\mathbf{x_\mathrm{sin,a}} = (x_1 + sin(0), x_2 + sin(1), \ldots, x_N + sin(N)) \in \mathbb{R}^N\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation}\mathbf{x_sin,a} = (x_1 + sin(0), x_2 + sin(1), \ldots, x_N + sin(N)) \in R^N\end{equation}
elements.subsection-2411-018: Concatenating polynomial PE to original spectrum
elements.sent-2411-484: We present the ``concatenating polynomial'' positional encoding technique, where we explore polynomial representation and evaluate the model performance for a second-order polynomial feature ($\alpha = 2$).
elements.sent-2411-485: The generation of the polynomial feature vector from the original spectrum is expressed as: \begin{equation}\mathbf{x_\mathrm{poly, c}} = \mathbf{x_\mathrm{original}} \odot \mathbf{x_\mathrm{original}} = (x^2_1, x^2_2, \ldots, x^2_N) \in \mathbb{R}^N\end{equation} The resulting polynomial positional encoding vector, $\mathbf{x_\mathrm{poly, c }$, is then concatenated with the original vector along the velocity axis.}
elements.sent-2411-486: This produces a matrix of size ($2 \times N$), where the first row represents the original spectrum $\mathbf{x_\mathrm{original = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N)$, and the second row contains the polynomial positional encoding vector $\mathbf{x_\mathrm{poly, c = (x^2_1, x^2_2, \ldots, x^2_N)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-487: The final model input is represented by the ensuing matrix: \begin{equation}\mathbf{X}_\mathrm{poly,c} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{x_\mathrm{original}} \mathbf{x_\mathrm{poly,c}} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_{1} x_{2} \ldots x_{N} x^2_1 x^2_2 \ldots x^2_N \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times N}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation}X_poly,c =  \mathbf{x_original} \mathbf{x_poly,c}  =  x_{1} x_{2} \ldots x_{N} x^2_1 x^2_2 \ldots x^2_N  \in R^{2 \times N}\end{equation}
elements.subsection-2411-019: Adding learnable PE to original spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-488: The learnable positional encoding (LPE) allows deep learning models to learn positional information during training.
elements.sent-2411-489: Instead of using fixed predefined positional encodings, we use learnable encoding for each position in the spectral input.
elements.sent-2411-490: The learnable vector is treated as additional parameters, and their values are optimized along with the rest of the model parameters during the training process.
elements.sent-2411-491: While LPE introduces more flexibility, it also increases the number of parameters in the models for training, which can affect training and generalization.
elements.sent-2411-492: Therefore, we only assess the learnable positional encoding to our \ourmod.
elements.sent-2411-493: In terms of implementation, the learnable positional encoding is often added to the input.
elements.sent-2411-494: The LPE parameters are initialized randomly from a random distribution, such as a normal distribution $\mathcal{N (0, \sigma^2)$ with a mean $\mu = 0$ and a small standard deviation $\sigma$.}
elements.sent-2411-495: Mathematically, the learnable positional encoding can be represented as a vector: \begin{equation}\mathbf{p} = (p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_N) \in \mathbb{R}^N \label{pe:lpe}\end{equation} with each $p_i \sim \mathcal{N (0, \sigma^2)$.}
elements.sent-2411-496: Subsequently, this learnable positional encoding vector is added element--wise to the original spectrum vector $\mathbf{x_\mathrm{original = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N)$ to form the final model input vector: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathbf{x_\mathrm{lpe}} = \mathbf{x_\mathrm{original}} + \mathbf{p} = (x_1 + p_1, x_2 + p_2, \ldots, x_N + p_N) \in \mathbb{R}^N \end{aligned}\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-019: \begin{equation} \mathbf{x_lpe} = \mathbf{x_original} + p = (x_1 + p_1, x_2 + p_2, \ldots, x_N + p_N) \in R^N \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-497: We train our CNN and \ourmod\ models on synthetic spectral cubes, experimenting with diverse data representations by combining original data with positional encodings as the model inputs.
elements.sent-2411-498: While evaluating our CNN performance with six absolute positional encoding techniques: ``original spectrum'', ``concat index'', ``add index'', ``concat sinusoidal'', ``add sinusoidal'' and ``concat second-order polynomial'', we are keen to examine \ourmod\ models with an additional data representation: ``learnable'' positional encoding.
elements.sent-2411-499: The parameters of LPE can be learned during the training phase.
elements.sent-2411-500: Yet, it adds more free parameters to the models, thus requiring more computational resources and training time.
elements.sent-2411-501: The models might opt to fine-tune the parameters of the positional vector to reduce the loss function instead of optimizing the parameters in the neural network.
elements.sent-2411-502: This may cause a reduction in performance.
elements.sent-2411-503: Absolute positional encodings provide information about the absolute position or order of elements in a sequence \cite[e.g.,][]{Vaswani2017,Devlin2018,Kitaev2020,Liu2019,Dufter2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-504: These encodings are typically incorporated into models either by addition (``add index'', ``add sinusoidal'', ``learnable'') or concatenation (``concat index'', ``concat sinusoidal'', ``concat second-order polynomial'') with the original data.
elements.sent-2411-505: This integration enables the models to distinguish between different positions in the sequence.
elements.sent-2411-506: In natural language processing, for example, when processing a sentence, absolute positional encodings help the models understand the order of words in a sentence.
elements.sent-2411-507: Refer to Appendix \ref{sec:data_repr for more details on the positional encoding techniques employed in this work.}
elements.figure-2411-023: \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{fig/mse_vt_cnn_cnntrans_cube.png} \caption{Swarm-box plots testing RMSE values obtained from ten-trial training sessions of CNN (cyan) and \ourmod\ (black) models. The models utilize different positional encoding techniques on spectral PPV cubes.} \label{fig:mse_vt_cnn_cnntrans_cube}
elements.figure-2411-024: \includegraphics[width=1.\textwidth]{fig/cnn_train_valid_rmse_corr_split_1split.png} \caption{Correlations between validation and training RMSE values obtained from CNN models across positional encodings, employing the same training/validation/testing (top panels) and different shuffling setups (bottom panels). Each panel includes Spearman's correlation coefficient $R_\mathrm{Sp}$ (all p-values $< 10^{-10}$). The axis scales in all panels are identical.} \label{fig:cnn_train_valid_rmse_corr_split_1split}
elements.subsection-2411-020: \ourmod\ with added sinusoidal positions is the best model
elements.sent-2411-508: We compare the performances of \ourmod\ with deep CNNs using their testing RMSEs.
elements.sent-2411-509: To emphasize the importance of the representation learning method (in extracting the compact features of \hi\ emission spectra), we ensured that the models have nearly identical numbers of learnable parameters and similar hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-510: Figure \ref{fig:mse_vt_cnn_cnntrans_cube summarises the testing RMSEs from ten-training trials for the two deep learning architectures: CNN in cyan and \ourmod\ in black.}
elements.sent-2411-511: Dotted lines connect the median RMSEs from each positional encoding method.
elements.sent-2411-512: It is observed that self-attention-based models consistently achieve ($\sim$8\%) lower testing RMSEs than CNNs across all positional encodings, although \ourmod\ RMSEs slightly vary over a wider range.
elements.sent-2411-513: Among the CNN models alone, those incorporating positional encodings outperform the one (labeled as ``original'') without positional encoding.
elements.sent-2411-514: However, in the case of \ourmod\ models, RMSEs between various data representations do not show a clear trend.
elements.sent-2411-515: This implies that the \ourmod\ models seem able to mitigate the influence of positional encodings on their performance.
elements.sent-2411-516: To assess how sensitive the positional encodings are to weight initialization and shuffling configurations.
elements.sent-2411-518: In the first setup, we maintain the same training/validation/testing shuffle but different initial neural network weights, ensuring that the way we shuffle and partition our entire dataset into training, validation, and testing sets remains identical throughout.
elements.sent-2411-519: In the second setup, we randomly shuffle the entire dataset differently before splitting it into training, validation, and test sets in each run.

elements.subsection-2411-021: Impact of kernel sizes on CNN performance
elements.sent-2411-545: In a CNN, the convolutional layers indeed play a crucial role in extracting features of the input data.
elements.sent-2411-546: These layers use filters (i.e., convolutional kernels) of various sizes to convolve over the input and capture features at different scales \citep[e.g.,][]{Szegedy2015,Szegedy2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-547: Small kernel sizes (e.g., 3$\times$3, 5$\times$5 for 2D image processing) are commonly used for capturing fine-grained features in the input data, effectively learning local patterns.
elements.sent-2411-548: On the other hand, large kernel sizes (e.g., 9$\times$9, 11$\times$11) are employed to capture larger patterns and global features in the input data.
elements.sent-2411-549: Here, we conduct a simple experiment to examine the impact of kernel sizes on the performance of our convolutional network.
elements.sent-2411-550: Namely, we train an 8-layer CNN model without positional encoding using varying kernel sizes (3, 9, 17, 25, 35, 39, and 45 velocity channels) in the first convolutional layer, while keeping the parameters of the other layers unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-551: The RMSE error for each kernel size is shown in Figure \ref{fig:rmse_kernel_size .}
elements.sent-2411-552: Initially, the RMSE decreases with increasing kernel size, reaching a minimum at 39 spectral channels, equivalent to a 12\% improvement.
elements.sent-2411-553: However, beyond this point, as kernel sizes continue to widen, RMSE starts to increase.
elements.sent-2411-554: The spectral width of 39 channels is about $\sim$10 \kms, the typical width of a WNM component, and approximately four times the typical width of a CNM component.
elements.sent-2411-555: This observation suggests that both very narrow and overly wide spectral kernels may struggle to adequately capture local/global features within the input.
elements.sent-2411-556: While narrow spectral filters (e.g., 3, 5, or 7 velocity channels) might cause CNNs to learn primarily the fluctuation of noise features, wide kernel windows may lead to potential oversmoothing.
elements.sent-2411-557: It is important to note that wider kernel sizes require significantly more time for training.
elements.sent-2411-558: For instance, training a CNN model with a kernel size of 35 channels takes ten times longer than with a kernel size of nine channels, yet with $\sim$6\% improvement in RMSE error.
elements.sent-2411-559: Considering training time and model performance, we design our models to iteratively use two kernel sizes, (1$\times$7) and (1$\times$33), for convolutional layers to capture \hi\ features at various spectral scales.
elements.sent-2411-560: When shallow CNNs (without positional encoding) are applied to an observed dataset where the detected signal is not centralized in the center of the spectral velocity ranges, they may malfunction, resulting in blank maps with all pixel values (\FCNM\ or \RHI) set to zero, as reported by \cite{Marchal2024 (see their Section 8).}
elements.sent-2411-561: In contrast, \ourmod\ models demonstrate the capability to produce results comparable to those obtained from the ROHSA Gaussian decomposition (see Section \ref{sec:application_to_emission ).}
elements.sent-2411-562: This emphasizes the important roles played by the combination of positional encodings, CNN-based feature extraction, and the Transformer self-attention mechanism in spectral data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-563: According to recent research by \cite{Gulati2020 , \cite{Pan2022 , and \cite{Rozanski2023 , CNNs are crucial to capturing local spectral features and extracting compact patterns from the input data, whereas the Transformer self-attention focuses on the global spectral patterns.}}}
elements.sent-2411-564: In particular, \cite{Pan2022 used Astroconformer, a convolution-augmented Transformer model, to analyze stellar spectroscopic data, highlighting the important function of Transformer blocks in capturing long-range information embedded within spectra.}
elements.sent-2411-565: Through comprehensive ablation studies, they found that CNNs do not outperform their baseline Multilayer Perceptron counterparts in analyzing the intrinsic complexity of stellar spectra.
elements.sent-2411-566: With the use of the hybrid convolutional-Transformer model, this may provide a potential improvement of the high latitude \FCNM\ map produced by \cite{Hensley2022 using M20 CNN and recently adopted by \cite{Lei2023 .}}

The corresponding spectral widths are illustrated on the top axis. The channel width is 0.3125 \kms.} \label{fig:rmse_kernel_size}
elements.subsection-2411-022: Impacts of spectral resolutions and noise levels on CNM fraction prediction
elements.figure-2411-029: \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{fig/spectral_res.png} \caption{Swarm-box plots for the testing RMSEs from \ourmod\ models employing various positional encoding techniques across different numbers of spectral channels. The spectral resolutions include 101 channels with 0.8 \kms\ spectral width and 256 channels with 0.3125 \kms\ spectral width, using PPV spectral datacubes. The annotations ``256$\rightarrow$101'' and ``101$\rightarrow$256'' refer to regridding from 256 channels to 101 channels and vice versa.} \label{fig:spectral_res}
elements.sent-2411-567: Throughout this paper, we consistently utilize training spectral datacubes consisting of 256 spectral channels, corresponding to a spectral resolution of 0.3125 \kms, to train the \ourmods.
elements.sent-2411-568: However, we further investigate the impact of spectral resolutions on the performance of the models in this subsection.
elements.sent-2411-569: In Figure \ref{fig:spectral_res , swarm-box plots display test RMSE values for various positional encoding techniques across different numbers of spectral channels.}
elements.sent-2411-570: The spectral resolutions under examination include 0.8 \kms\ with 101 channels and 0.3125 \kms\ with 256 channels.
elements.sent-2411-571: The annotations ``256$\rightarrow$101'' and ``101$\rightarrow$256'' denote regridding from 256 channels to 101 channels and vice versa (to mimic a fabricated spectral resolution downgrade/upgrade, respectively).
elements.sent-2411-572: Across all cases of positional encodings, higher spectral resolution with 256 velocity channels results in lower RMSE error compared to 101 velocity channels.
elements.sent-2411-573: In addition, regridding from 101 channels to 256 channels or vice versa does not enhance the models' performances.
elements.sent-2411-574: This emphasizes the significance of genuine increased spectral resolution as inputs to models.
elements.sent-2411-575: Nonetheless, \hi\ spectral data with high spectral resolutions typically need a greater number of spectral channels (e.g., 512, 1024, or 2048 channels), unavoidably leading to more complex model architectures and, as a result, requiring more time for training and testing processes.
elements.sent-2411-576: Hence, when utilizing the ``tano signal'' package, which can automatically construct an appropriate \ourmod\ model based on spectral inputs, users are advised to carefully select a spectral resolution and spectral length that align with the scientific requirements.
elements.sent-2411-577: In practice, it would be ideal to systematically explore various parameters -- such as beam size, noise level, and spatial resolution -- and train deep learning models on a synthetic database with parameters closely matching those of the observations.
elements.sent-2411-578: However, due to limitations in high-performance computing resources, instead of investigating a broad range of observing parameters, we introduced a relatively high noise level of 0.5 -- 0.8 K and smoothed the training data cubes with a beam size of 45 arcsec (as described in Section \ref{subsec:training_datasets ).}
elements.sent-2411-579: Despite these constraints, the models have demonstrated promising performance when applied to different observed datasets with varying noise levels and spatial resolutions.
elements.sent-2411-580: The results remain reasonable when compared to other methods (see Sections \ref{sec:compare_with_absorption and \ref{sec:application_to_emission for details), indicating that the model can generalize well across different observational conditions.}}
elements.sent-2411-581: This robustness aligns with the principles of 21-cm radiative transfer, where the contributions of CNM gas -- characterized by low temperatures and high optical depth -- to emission profiles are pronounced and primarily dominate the brightness temperature peaks \citep[see ][]{Murray2015,Murray2018,Marchal2024,Nguyen2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-582: For instance, an \hi\ parcel with a temperature of \Ts\ = 50 K and an optical depth $\tau$ = 0.1 would have a brightness temperature $T_\mathrm{b}$ $\sim$ 5 K, which is above the 3$\sigma$ detection limit of existing \hi\ surveys.
elements.sent-2411-583: Furthermore, during the development of the Bayesian version of \ourmod\ (Muller et al., in preparation), we observed that noise levels barely affected the CNM fraction prediction.
elements.sent-2411-584: Namely, increased noise led to slightly more scatter in the one-to-one comparisons between ground truths and predictions.
elements.sent-2411-585: On the other hand, predictions for UNM and WNM fractions were marginally more sensitive to noise (and the physical scales that the models are trained on), due to the typically lower $T_\mathrm{b}$ values of these components.
elements.sent-2411-586: For instance, a UNM cloud with a temperature of \Ts\ = 1000 K and an optical depth $\tau$ = 10$^{-3}$ would have a brightness temperature $T_\mathrm{b}$ $\sim$ 1 K.
elements.sent-2411-587: If the noise level exceeds 1 K per velocity channel, it would introduce more uncertainty into the predictions for UNM and WNM fractions.. \bsp.
